---
title: "Verify"
---

`Verify` is the primitive that decides whether a task is done. It is total: it
returns a green or a red, never a shrug. This chapter is the canonical verify
sequence and the reporting shape that surfaces its result honestly.

## The standard sequence

After any code change, the verification is fixed and ordered. Each step is an
inspect or a verify; the only mutation is entering play mode.

```{.cli name="Standard verify sequence"}
unity-cli editor refresh --force --compile
echo 'return 42;' | unity-cli exec
unity-cli console --type error
unity-cli console --clear
unity-cli editor play
echo 'return UnityEngine.Time.time;' | unity-cli exec
unity-cli console --type error
```

The reconnect probe after refresh exists because refresh can return
inconclusively. The two `console --type error` reads bracket play mode: clean
before, clean after. A red console at either end means not done.

::: {.failure title="Never use --wait on play with a possible compile error"}
A compile error stalls a waited play-mode entry indefinitely. Enter play
without waiting, then verify runtime state with a separate `exec`.
:::

## Verify the specific effect

A clean console is necessary, not sufficient. The task's actual effect must be
verified by name. For ECS work that almost always means an entity count.

```{.exec name="Verify baking produced entities" usings="Unity.Entities,Unity.Transforms"}
var em = World.DefaultGameObjectInjectionWorld.EntityManager;
var t = System.Type.GetType("Unity.Transforms.LocalToWorld, Unity.Transforms");
var q = em.CreateEntityQuery(t);
var n = q.CalculateEntityCount();
return n > 0 ? $"OK: {n} entities" : "FAIL: zero entities";
```

::: {.verify title="The green that counts"}
The query returns a positive count. This is the verify that closes the bracket
for a baking task — not "the command returned," not "the console is clean,"
but the named effect, measured.
:::

## Reporting

Report in a fixed order so a reader can audit the claim, not just trust it.

::: {.axiom title="The reporting order"}
1. what was inspected
2. what was changed
3. compilation result
4. direct verification result
5. remaining blocker, if any
:::

Never compress these into a vague success statement. "Working now" hides which
of the five was actually checked. The five-line report is longer and that is the
point: it is falsifiable. A stranger can re-run step four and see the same green.

## The anti-patterns verify exists to catch

| Disguise | Why it is red | The real verify |
|---|---|---|
| Manual entity creation | proves nothing about baking | query the baked entities |
| A passing test wrapping a stub | tests the stub, not the system | run the system, count the effect |
| `connection closed` read as success | inconclusive, not done | reconnect probe, then re-query |
| Listing `world.Systems` for an `ISystem` | unmanaged systems are absent | verify by query effect |
